problem_summary: 2019 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability/environment): build a model that internalizes ecosystem-service / environmental cost into the cost-benefit analysis (CBA) of land-use development projects across project sizes and time horizons. This O-award team framed it as an environmental-economics externality problem: the "true" cost of a land-use project = economic cost + environmental cost, and standard CBA ignores the latter. They built two coupled models — a short-run monetized model and a long-run multi-criteria (non-monetized) model — and validated each on a real case (a small Chinese paper mill; the TVA Tennessee Valley thermal-power buildout 1940–1965).
models_used: Short-run environmental-cost model via the REPLACEMENT/RESTORATION COST METHOD: EC = C_RS*(P_S-LP_S) + C_RW*(P_W-LP_W) + C_RA*(P_A-LP_A), pricing pollution above nature's self-purification capacity (LP) at unit treatment cost for solid/water/air; total cost C_S = PC (economic) + EC.

Discounted cash-flow benefit model: economic benefit EB = sum_{t=0}^{T} B_t/(1+r)^t (present value over project life at social discount rate r), plus social benefit terms B21 (employment-rate monetary value) + B22 (city-image value).

Long-run cost model via Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), 3-layer (goal / criteria [economic vs environmental cost, weighted 1/2:1/2] / alternatives), because long-run degradation costs (life-quality LWC + sustainable-development SDC + ecology ELC) resist direct monetization.

Five environmental indicators feeding the AHP alternatives layer: Simpson Diversity Index (SDI) for biodiversity; Air Quality Index (AQI = max over pollutant sub-indices P_i, linear interpolation between concentration breakpoints) for air; NDVI = (NIR-R)/(NIR+R) as a proxy for soil quality; Ross Water Quality Index (weighted-mean WQI) for water; Environmental Health Indicator (EHI, 0-100) for human-health feeling.
AHP pairwise comparison matrix J (5x5) with eigenvalue/eigenvector weighting, consistency test (CI=(lambda-n)/(n-1), CR=CI/RI, accept if CR<0.1).

Ordinal scoring/ranking scheme (Table 2): index changes binned into 5 ranks scored 9/7/5/3/1, with economic cost benchmarked against industry average cost AC; total cost = inner product of rank vector and combination weight vector, compared to similarly-scored total benefit.

One-parameter sensitivity analysis on the criteria-layer weight a (economy vs environment), yielding cost-score as a linear function of a (e.g., 0.295a+4.705 after optimization vs 0.858a+4.142 before).
key_techniques: Two-tier model design keyed to the prompt's own axes (time scale + project size): a monetized short-run model for small/current-impact projects and a non-monetized AHP model for large/long-horizon projects — directly mirroring the question's 'varying sizes and time' requirement.

Honest handling of the monetization wall: rather than force fake dollar values on biodiversity/ecology, they switched methodology to AHP ordinal scoring, then made cost and benefit comparable by scoring BOTH on the same 1-9 rank scale against industry average cost AC.

Novel, transparent prior for the AHP matrix: estimating relative index importance from Google Scholar literature counts (5.54M 'biodiversity', 3.18M 'air quality', etc.) — a reproducible if crude proxy for 'societal attention' when no survey data exists.

Matrix re-optimization per case: replacing the generic consistent matrix J0 with a case-tuned J1 (up-weighting air quality for a coal thermal plant), re-running the consistency test (lambda=5.04, CR passes), demonstrating the model adapts to project type.
Replacement-cost method anchored on nature's self-purification capacity (charging only pollution ABOVE carrying capacity P-LP), giving the environmental-cost term a clear physical/ecological meaning.

Closed-loop validation against known real-world outcomes: paper mill rejected (matches China's actual crackdown on small mills); TVA thermal benefit >> cost (matches its historically positive value) — using reality as the ground-truth check on the model.
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat, this paper wins on problem-framing maturity and methodological honesty rather than mathematical heavyweight machinery. It reads the sustainability prompt as an environmental-externality/CBA problem and answers it precisely on the prompt's own terms — two models indexed to time-scale and project-size, each demonstrated on a concrete real case. The standout judging signal is the team's intellectual honesty at the monetization boundary: they openly state that long-run ecological costs can't be credibly priced, and instead of fabricating dollar figures they pivot to AHP ordinal scoring and explicitly make cost and benefit commensurable on one scale. Judges reward that kind of self-aware methodology choice. The Google-Scholar-count prior for the AHP matrix is a clever, reproducible answer to the perennial 'where do your weights come from' question, and they immediately stress-test it with a consistency check, a per-case re-optimized matrix, and a clean one-parameter sensitivity analysis showing the decision is robust to the economy-vs-environment weighting. Finally, both cases are validated against actual historical outcomes (small mills shut down; TVA was a net win), so the model isn't just internally consistent — it reproduces reality. Strong structure, real data, candid weaknesses section, and a clear policy takeaway (Pigovian tax / internalize the externality) round out a complete, defensible submission."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet is tight and decision-oriented: states both models, both cases, and both verdicts (mill rejected, TVA approved) in one page. Section 4 builds the models progressively — short-run basic model first (4.1), then 'improved' long-run model (4.2) — with clean tree/flowchart figures (Figs 1-3) decomposing cost and benefit into labeled sub-components, which makes the notation easy to follow. The AHP development is well-staged across the three layers (alternatives -> criteria -> goal) with the consistency test stated explicitly. Case studies (Sec 5) are vivid and concrete: the paper mill is told as 'a story' with named assumptions and a single decisive inequality (11.2M yuan environmental cost >> benefit); the TVA case is data-grounded with sourced capital/revenue tables in the appendix and a per-case matrix re-optimization (5.2.4). Sensitivity analysis (Sec 6) reduces to a clean linear score-vs-weight plot and the memorable conclusion that 'the decision to build will always be made regardless of emphasis.' A dedicated 'Implications for planners/managers' section (Sec 7) translates results into policy (regulation, Pigovian tax, resident surveys for the matrix). The Strengths/Weaknesses section is unusually candid — it admits the matrix prior is rough, the long/short boundary is vague, and that they under-developed the benefit/environmental-benefit side. MATLAB code for the AHP eigenvector + consistency test and the sensitivity plot is included in the appendix for reproducibility."
pitfalls_or_limitations: AHP weighting prior is weak: deriving index importance from raw Google Scholar hit counts conflates research volume with societal/ecological importance; the team itself flags it as 'relatively rough' and suggests resident polls instead.

Ordinal 9/7/5/3/1 scoring loses magnitude information — when benefit hugely exceeds cost (TVA: benefit 7,280M vs AC 3,000M), everything saturates at rank 9 and the score cannot express the size of the gap (acknowledged weakness).

Mixing incommensurable cost types under one AHP rank scale is convenient but conceptually shaky: a monetized capital cost and an ecological index change are forced onto the same 1-9 ladder via subjective rank bins (Table 2 thresholds are asserted, not derived).

Short-run case is thin/back-of-envelope: only COD water pollution is priced for the paper mill, many parameters are assumed, and one citation claims an implausible '1 million cubic meters of water per ton of paper' (almost certainly a units/typo error carried into the narrative).
Benefit side is under-modeled relative to cost — no environmental BENEFIT (e.g., green-project ecosystem gains) is included, which the team admits limits applicability to genuinely green projects.
Long-run vs short-run boundary is never formally defined (admitted), so which model applies to a given project is left to judgment; each case is run through only one model rather than both.

Several derivations are loosely presented: the CI formula is written CI=(lambda-r)/(r-1) with 'r' standing in for matrix size n, NDVI is used as a soil-quality proxy without justification of that substitution, and EHI/WQI computations are described qualitatively rather than fully worked.
reusable_patterns: Two-model split along the prompt's stated dimensions: when a problem asks for 'varying sizes / different time scales,' build one model per regime (here monetized short-run vs AHP long-run) instead of one over-general model — it directly answers the question and gives two demonstrations.

Monetize-when-you-can, score-when-you-can't: use replacement/restoration cost (or willingness-to-pay) for tractable items, and switch to AHP ordinal scoring for intangibles — then put both on a common rank scale so they remain comparable in the final CBA.

Replacement-cost-above-carrying-capacity template: price only the externality that exceeds nature's self-purification (P - LP) at unit treatment cost — a clean, defensible way to attach dollars to pollution.

Transparent data-driven prior for subjective weights: when no survey exists, derive AHP pairwise importances from a reproducible external signal (literature counts, search volume, citation counts) and always follow with the CR<0.1 consistency test.

Per-case matrix re-optimization: keep a generic baseline comparison matrix, then re-tune weights to the specific project's dominant impact (air for a coal plant) and re-verify consistency — shows the framework generalizes.

One-parameter sensitivity reduced to a closed-form line: parameterize the key subjective weight (economy-vs-environment a in [0,1]), express the output score as a linear function of it, and plot before/after to argue robustness of the decision.

Reality as validation oracle: pick cases whose real-world outcome is already known (a banned small mill, a historically successful TVA) and show the model reproduces those verdicts — cheap, persuasive validation when ground truth exists.
Ship reproducible code: include the MATLAB AHP eigenvector + consistency-test routine and the sensitivity-plot script in an appendix so judges can audit the numbers.
